text: The patient is positioned in a left lateral decubitus position. The Da Vinci X robotic cart is docked from the patient's head. Four robotic ports are placed along the virtual subcostal lobotomy line, and two laparoscopic ports are positioned across the umbilicus and in the left flank. The second surgeon stands between the patient's legs.

The surgeon mobilizes the right lobe of the liver, performing a section of the suprahepatic vein using a robotic hook and bipolar forceps. The posterior glissonian pedicle is visible. A cholecystectomy is performed, with the cystic duct and artery being sectioned between amyloid clips.
The surgeon uses the robotic force arm to aid in exposure, sectioning the right coronary ligament to access the tripartite inferior vena cava.

The surgeon dissects accessory supra-hepatic veins, sectioning larger veins between Hem-o-lok clips and coagulating smaller veins with bipolar forceps. The robotic arm aids in exposing the entire face of the inferior vena cava, facilitating the dissection and control of the veins using robotic bipolar and hook instruments.
A branch for segment VII is dissected and sectioned between two hemlock clips, with visible control and precision in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the right lobe by sectioning the superior part of the right triangular ligament, ensuring full access to the liver structure.
The surgeon performs intraoperative ultrasonography to visualize the glycine branch of segment VI and segment VII, displaying the anatomical structures on the ultrasound screen.
The surgeon begins with a caudate lobe first approach, sectioning the caudate lobe along the axis of the inferior vena cava, revealing the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon at the robotic console utilizes a robotic hook and bipolar forceps, while the assisting surgeon employs a vacuum irrigator to clean the surgical field and irrigate the bipolar forceps.
At the end of the caudate lobe transection, the glissane branches of segment VI and segment VII are visible. A surgical loop is placed around the segment VII glissane branch.
The surgeon performs a clamping test to ensure the demarcation line and checks for portal flow in the anterior section. The segment VII glissane branch is sectioned between two Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon continues the anatomical resection of segment seven using Prankemaltransiction. The bipolar forceps are irrigated to enhance the efficiency of bipolar energy, preventing coagulated tissue from adhering to the forceps and tearing delicate vessels, while maintaining a clear view of the forceps' tips.

The surgeon continues parenchymal transection along the axis of the right portal sulcus, identifying the right hepatic vein. The vein is dissected on its right side until it approaches the inferior vena cava. An ultrasonic dissector is used for its atraumatic properties during this part of the procedure.

The robotic console surgeon utilizes arm four and the second surgeon's three hands to facilitate parenchymal transection with four hands. Hemostasis and biliostasis are achieved using bipolar forceps and a hook, ensuring controlled bleeding and bile leakage.
The surgeon dissects branches of segment VII leading to the right suprahepatic vein and sections them between hematoclips to achieve hemostasis.

The robotic devices are articulated to maintain alignment with the right hepatic veins. A surgical loop is positioned behind the transection line to enhance exposure at the end of the parenchymal transection.

The surgeon completes the dissection, repairing bleeding from the right hepatic vein with sutures. The stable vision and precise control facilitate bleeding management. The resection reveals the suprahepatic vein, segment VII glissadine branch, and the inferior vena cava.
Postoperatively, a drainage tube is positioned behind the liver, and the surgical specimen is removed through a pharyngeal incision using a handbag technique.
